frameworks.name: Bayesian perceptual inference
frameworks.one_line: Observer combines longer-term priors with immediate context to infer scene orientation.

frameworks.name: natural image statistics prior
frameworks.one_line: Prior over low-level image statistics aggregated across many natural scenes.

frameworks.name: context-dependent prior re-weighting
frameworks.one_line: Prior weight is rapidly adjusted by immediate contextual information, even when misleading.

core_question: How do observers trade off long-term natural-image-statistic priors against immediate contextual information when judging upright orientation of naturalistic scenes?
key_assumptions: Perceptual priors arise from statistical regularities of natural environments
Priors operate in conjunction with context-specific information
Observers can be modeled as Bayesian inference machines over low-level image statistics
manipulations: Naturalistic scene images requiring upright-orientation judgement
Perceptual inference using internal criteria rather than comparative judgement
key_findings: Observer performance is well approximated by a model using a prior over aggregated low-level image statistics
Dependence on the long-term prior is rapidly re-weighted by immediate contextual information
Re-weighting occurs even when the contextual information is misleading
interpretations: Visuo-cognitive function tolerates frequent prior violations by flexibly combining priors with context-specific evidence
Low-level image statistics form a usable prior for inference on naturalistic stimuli without explicit comparison
